query: CT scan findings for extradural, subdural, intracerebral, and subarachnoid hemorrhages

retrieved_docs.title: Intracerebral Hemorrhage -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The primary feature of a SAH is the pathognomonic sudden onset, severe headache ‘like being hit at the back of the head.’ Apart from this feature, which may not always be expressed so eloquently, patients may present much the same way as those with acute ICH. In a SAH, an unenhanced CT Head would reveal blood within the subarachnoid space and ventricular cisterns rather than within the parenchyma, as seen in an ICH. An acute subdural hematoma may have similar symptoms. However, the key differentiating factor is a history of recent trauma preceding the presentation. Chronic subdural hemorrhages are most commonly seen in the elderly, particularly those on blood-thinning medication, and history is often of recurrent falls followed by a longer duration of headaches and/or confusion and/or focal neurological deficit. Both acute and chronic subdural hematomas can be differentiated on a plain CT head as a crescentic extra-axial collection- hypodense if the blood is chronic and hyperdense in the acute setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hemorrhage -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses of intracranial hemorrhage include: Infection: Subdural empyema may mimic a subdural hemorrhage Recent contrast administration Subdural hygroma: May appear similar to chronic subdural hemorrhage Tumors: Meningioma may mimic an extradural hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury -- Evaluation -- Computed Tomography Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Concurrent primary traumatic lesions often coexist with DAI on CT. In one series of DAI patients admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU), skull fractures were seen in 39% of cases, subarachnoid hemorrhage in 67%, intraventricular hemorrhage in 34%, cerebral contusions in 46%, cerebral edema in 31%, subdural hematomas in 25%, extradural hematomas in 12.9% and pneumocephalus in 8.9%. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Subarachnoid Hemorrhage(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, a non-contrast head CT is obtained during the presentation. [15] Almost 99% of cases are picked up on head CT if performed within a 6-hour window. [15] However, MRI is considered more sensitive than head CT as time goes on. [1] [16] However, If the non-contrast head CT is indeterminate or if the patient presents outside of the 6-hour window, a lumbar puncture (LP) is indicated. [17] Some studies have found lumbar puncture to show evidence of hemorrhage in 3% of patients with a normal head CT. [17] Conversely, a recent study concluded that LP, in their cohort of neurologically intact CT-negative emergency department headache patients, did not identify any cases of aneurysmal SAH but was associated with serious complications, a significant false-positive rate, and extended emergency department length of stay. [17] Thus, attempts to investigate SAH (or sentinel bleeds from a presumed small aneurysmal leak) with a CT scan and lumbar puncture should be discussed with patients in a shared decision-making approach with informed consent/refusal.

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hemorrhage -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: A non-contrast computed tomography (CT) scan of the head is the imaging modality unless additional modalities are indicated for further evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Embolic Stroke -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Stroke mimics should be considered when evaluating patients with suspected embolic strokes in the Emergency department. A brain CT scan should be considered to exclude intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) in patients presenting with focal neurological symptoms. ICH includes intraparenchymal hemorrhage secondary to hypertension or amyloid angiopathy in addition to subarachnoid and subdural hematomas.

retrieved_docs.content: Stroke Spontaneous acute subdural hematoma Alzheimer disease Anterior circulation stroke Brain metastasis Cerebral aneurysms Frontal lobe syndrome Hydrocephalus

retrieved_docs.title: CT Scan -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Brain : for patients with hydrocephalus, the CT scan is the principal study to evaluate ventricle size and compare the size in cases of shunt malfunction. [62] For pediatric patients with headaches, loss of consciousness, or seizures, the CT scan is usually normal; thus, judicious use is encouraged to avoid radiation exposure. [62] For a patient involved in a traumatic scenario, the CT scan can show skull fractures, traumatic hematomas (epidural, subdural, and intracerebral), and edema in a fast way to obtain rapid management. CT scan can be used to detect spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage, hematomas, and stroke. Cerebral vascular lesions like arteriovenous malformations and aneurysms can be suggested due to the presence of calcifications. CT-perfusion is used to determine the cerebral blood flow to specific brain areas using color-coded maps. [63] CT angiography identifies whether there is adequate blood flow within respective organs such as the brain, heart, lung, kidney, or extremities. [64] [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Subfalcine Herniation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with signs or symptoms of neurologic deterioration require a thorough history taking and physical examination. The history may raise suspicion of intracranial lesions that might lead to neurological deficits. A head computed tomography (CT) scan is the preferred imaging study in TBI. If there is a suspicion of a brain tumor, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with and without contrast enhancement is preferred as it shows the lesion with better anatomic and spatial details and can also exclude other non-tumoral etiologies. All patients with neurological deficits require frequent re-evaluation. If any significant findings raise the suspicion of intracranial herniation, a non-contrast CT brain scan should be ordered immediately. The CT scan detects acute hemorrhagic lesions and subtle concurrent midline shifts. It is readily available in most trauma centers and provides rapid results which help improve patient prognosis and clinical outcome. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hemorrhage -- Evaluation -- Subdural Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: An acute subdural hematoma is typically hyperdense, while a chronic subdural is hypodense. A subacute subdural may be isodense to the brain and more challenging to identify (see Image. Subdural Hematoma Detected on Head CT).

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Hematoma -- Evaluation -- Computed Tomography
retrieved_docs.content: Head CT is the primary diagnostic modality for EDH due to its widespread availability and rapid image acquisition in emergency settings. Most EDHs are readily identifiable on noncontrast (CT computed tomography), typically as a biconvex or lens-shaped (lentiform) hyperdense mass. This shape reflects the limited blood expansion due to the dura's firm attachment at cranial sutures. EDH rarely crosses suture lines but may cross dural reflections such as the falx or tentorium, distinguishing it from subdural hematoma (SDH).

It is identified as a cause of الخرف من نوع الزهايمر (Alzheimer‑type dementia), of headache—including migraine—, of nervous system congenital anomalies, and of نزيف دموي تحت الجافية (subdural hemorrhage). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 234
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging pathways are organized around the routine chest X‑ray, which serves as a reference standard for a wide range of computed tomography (CT) examinations. From the chest X‑ray, CT protocols are mapped to chest CT, abdominal CT (التصوير المقطعي للبطن), combined chest‑abdomen, chest‑abdomen‑pelvis, brain‑chest, and brain‑chest‑abdomen studies, with or without intravenous contrast. The brain component of these combined studies follows the standard head CT protocol (computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head) and belongs to central nervous system imaging procedures (إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي). Encounters generated because of a routine chest X‑ray are explicitly related to that imaging study, underscoring its role as the methodological basis for subsequent advanced CT imaging. Collectively, these relationships integrate neurological disorders (hydrocephalus, dementia, seizures, dizziness), their neurosurgical interventions (ventricular shunts), underlying etiologies

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1675
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are specific imaging studies that are primary‑mapped from the generic procedure computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. The CT scan head itself is classified under the broader category إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system imaging procedures). Within this parent category, there are additional child procedures, including مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي, indicating that the hierarchy of CNS imaging encompasses both standard CT head scans and related specialized studies such as the brain‑and‑chest CT examinations and abnormal brain echo mapping. This organization highlights how individual CT examinations are derived from a core CT head modality and situated within the overall framework of CNS imaging for comprehensive patient assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are both procedures that are primarily mapped from the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head, indicating that the head CT protocol serves as the foundational reference for these combined brain and chest imaging studies. The version with intravenous contrast medium incorporates contrast administration to enhance visualization of structures, while both examinations rely on the same underlying CT head imaging principles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1011
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is classified as an إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury) and is associated with an إصابة داخلية (internal injury). Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are identified as types of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, indicating that skull‑base fractures are encompassed within the broader category of intracranial injuries. Additionally, إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is recognized as a subtype of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, further linking closed traumatic brain events to the same injury classification. Together, these relationships illustrate that various traumatic brain conditions—including subarachnoid hemorrhage from an open wound, anterior and middle fossa fractures, and closed head trauma—are grouped under the umbrella of intracranial injury, underscoring their interconnected nature for diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 828
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head serves as the primary reference for more extensive contrast‑enhanced examinations, including computerized tomography of the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium and computerized tomography of the brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These head CT studies are clinically associated with خرف (dementia) and frequently co‑occur with خرف شيخوخي (senile or age‑related dementia). Consequently, contrast‑enhanced CT imaging of the brain—often performed together with chest and abdominal evaluation—plays a key role in the diagnostic assessment and monitoring of patients with dementia, particularly the senile form, highlighting the importance of head CT in the work‑up of cognitive decline.
